As network-based computational techniques progress, molecular networks offer significant clues for forecasting disease-associated genes. Nonetheless, many approaches overlook brain imaging data despite its frequent application in neuroscience studies. This study introduces the Deep Interactive Auto Encoder (DIAU), a new system that merges brain imaging with molecular gene networks to forecast brain disease genes . First, it creates a gene association graph derived from brain scans and comprehensive whole-brain transcriptomic data. Next, it applies an advanced multi-network fusion strategy to derive compact gene representations by integrating the imaging-derived graph with additional molecular gene graphs. These compact representations are then fed into a support vector regression (SVR) classifier to predict disease genes . The framework's efficacy was assessed against three leading methodologies across four distinct neurological conditions, revealing that the proposed approach outperforms existing solutions and underscores the value of incorporating imaging data into gene prediction models .

original: Recent advances in spatial omics technologies have allowed various omics data to be obtained from a single tissue section. To fully explore the relationships among these different types of omics data, it is urgent to develop more effective methods for spatial multi-omics data integration. In this work, we propose a novel Multi-level Graph Contrastive Learning framework, named SMMGCL, to simultaneously mine complementary information at both spot and graph levels for integrating Spatial Multi-omics data. Specifically, to adaptively fuse multi-omics modalities, we first design a multi-modality autoencoder that integrates spatial locations with spot omic expressions to extract modality-specific embeddings. These embeddings are then fused into a consensus representation using an attention mechanism to capture spot-level cross-omics representations. Next, to explore the complex inter-omic structural information, we connect corresponding spots across different omics adjacency graphs into a heterogeneous graph. We then employ a graph convolutional network (GCN) to extract spatial correlations across the omics, learning a graph-level cross-omics global representation. Finally, SMMGCL aligns feature similarity matrixes between spot-level and graph-level representations with their pseudo-label similarity matrix, ensuring multi-level clustering consistency and leading to more accurate spatial multi-omics integration. Experimental results on simulated and real datasets from across tissues show that SMMGCL consistently outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in spatial multi-omics integration performance. The code for SMMGCL is available for download from the GitHub repository at https://github.com/cs-wangbo/SMMGCL.
rewritten: Recent developments in spatial omic technologies enable collection of multiple omic data from a single slide of tissue. To comprehensively investigate interrelationships between these diverse omic data types, there is a pressing need for improved spatial multi-omic integration methods. This study introduces SMMGCl, a Multi-level Graph Contrative Learning approach that concurrently extracts synergistic insights from spot and graph scales for Spatial Multi-omic integration. Initially, we construct a multi-modally aware autoencoder that merges spatial coordinates with spot-specific omic expression values to generate embeddings tailored to each modality. An attention-based fusion step consolidates these embeddings into a unified representation capturing spot-centric cross-omic patterns. Subsequently, we link matching spots across distinct omic adjacency networks to form a heterogenous graph. By applying a graph convolution network (GCn) we distill spatial interdependencies among the omics and derive a global cross-omic representation at the graph level. Lastly, SMMGC1 aligns the similarity matrices derived from spot- and graph-level embeddings with pseudo-label based similarity metrics, promoting consistent clustering across scales and enhancing the fidelity of spatial multi-omiic amalgamation. original: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) enables researchers to analyze gene expression at single-cell level. One important task in scRNA-seq data analysis is unsupervised clustering, which helps identify distinct cell types, laying down the foundation for other downstream analysis tasks. In this paper, we propose a novel method called Cluster-aware IteRative Contrastive LEarning (CIRCLE in short) for scRNA-seq data clustering, which uses an iterative representation learning and clustering framework to progressively learn the clustering structure of scRNA-seq data with a cluster-aware contrastive loss. CIRCLE consists of a Transformer encoder, a clustering head, a projection head and a contrastive loss. First, CIRCLE extracts the feature vectors of the original and augmented data by the Transformer-encoder. Then, it computes the clustering centroids by K-means and employs the student’s t-distribution to assign pseudo-labels to all cells in the clustering head. The projection-head uses a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) to obtain projections of the augmented data. At last, both pseudo-labels and projections are used in the contrastive loss to guide the model training. Such a process goes iteratively so that the clustering result gets better and better. Extensive experiments on 25 real-world scRNA-seq datasets show that CIRCLE outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in most cases. Concretely, CIRCLE surpasses the existing methods by from 14% to 280%, and from 5% to 133% on average in terms of performance metrics ARI and NMI respectively. Source code is available at https://github.com/Alunethy/CIRCLE.

We introduce a new technique named Cluster‑aware IteRactive Contrastive LEarninG (CIRCLE) for scDNA‑seq clustering that combines repeated representation learning with clustering using a cluster‑aware contrastive loss function. CIRCLE includes a Transformer encoder together with a clustering module, a projection module and a contrasti ve loss term. Initially, the Transformer encoder generates embeddings for both original and perturbed inputs. Next, K‑means determines cluster centroids and a Student's‑t distribution provides pseudo‑labels for each cell within the clustering module. An MLP‑based projection head creates representations for the augmented samples. Finally, pseudo‑labels and projected features feed into the contrastive objective to direct network optimisation. This cycle repeats, continually refining cluster assignments. Benchmarks across 25 public scRNA‑ seq datasets demonstrate CIRCLE's superiority over current best‑performing methods, improving ARI by 14‑280 % and NMI by 5‑133 % on average.
